sentences.text: Well, all I can say, I wish I wouldn't have to talk about it because that's the worst camp that I have been in my whole life through, while I was in the camp.
We arrive on-- we were traveling from Flossenburg to Mauthausen for 15 days in carriages without food.
It's unbelievable that a human being can go through such thing-- 15 days without food.
No food.
No food at all.
We were-- we were traveling back and forwards because the train lines were bumped.
So we couldn't reach the [? destination. ?] We were supposed to go to Mauthausen, but they couldn't reach there.
So people were dying on the way.
And we-- each station that had stopped, we were burying them.
I was once picked to bury those people.
I have seen people-- dead bodies in the camp.
I was-- I've seen lots and lots of them.
And I could never believe that I'd be able to do what I did when I went up to the wagon.
All those bodies that I've seen tangled together, I had to separate each one of them to bury-- to be buried.
I couldn't believe that I can do it.
But after doing a couple or so, I become inhuman.
I just-- it was just part of me.
I did it.
I was just doing it like I would play with toys.
I was undoing every [? bit, ?] and then we buried them.
I didn't cry.
I didn't do anything.
I just buried those bodies.
As we were traveling, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] one fellow escaped-- what-- was it one or two?
I don't remember it.
And when he escaped, as the train was going it cuts his hand and-- and legs off partly.
And the train stopped, and they brought-- this fellow was lying just there and still alive, blood everywhere.
And it was just unbelievable that they could leave him like this.
We begged them, why don't you kill him?
After a while, they shot him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And we still didn't know where we're going.
We were in our mind, if they-- if they want us to be alive, they would give us food.
That means we are going somewhere-- or just they're doing it on purpose so we'd be finished off.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When we were traveling for 15 days, we were very lucky, I must say again, because in my train where I was in the carriage there, there was with us a German who was a kapo in the camp.
He was one who didn't believe in Hitler as well, so he was in the concentration camp.
And another kapo was who was not supposed to be Jewish, but he was.
I will tell you after the story that I found out.
And they managed to, in Czechoslovakia when we were traveling back and forwards, on a station, they managed to buy a little bit of sugar in a bag.
And this sugar was given to us afterwards, every day a tiny little spoon like a teaspoon.
And so I can't say that I didn't eat for 15 days, because afterwards I had every day a little-- one little spoon of sugar.
That was my meal for 15 days.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] People from Czechoslovakia were coming close to our train nearly every day.
They found out that we are there in the train, and they begged them to give us food, because they-- they brought food.
All the Germans had to do give it to us.
They didn't allow them.
So they tried to come close to us with children, and maybe this way we'd be able to get something from them.
They send them back.
Oh, then on the end, they went and took the food and didn't give it to us.
So that was our transport.
And we-- every one of us wished to be dead, really, because it was just unbearable.
We could hardly breathe.
We could hardly walk.
It-- it was just unbelievable.
We arrived to Mauthausen after 15 days.
[CRYING] When we went off the train, you could see-- we-- we left Flossenburg 1,500 people.
We arrive to Mauthausen 470.
From those 470 people that we arrived-- we were walking not a long distant, very close-- people were dying just because they couldn't reach the camp.
But we were really sorry that it didn't happen to us as well, because we wished to be dead before we even arrived at the camp.
But we came into Mauthausen.
They took us straight into the gas chamber-- where the gas chamber was.
It was a big place there, and we were waiting to go into the gas chamber.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A friend of mine-- I wish I would remember his name.
I knew the two brothers extremely well.
And he came to me, and he recognize me.
Because he was working in the crematory.
He was-- after they were gassed, he used to dispose them.
He said, Maury, I will save your life.
I can do it.
I said, look, I really don't care.
I like to go now.
[CRYING] And I had this diamond ring with me.
I said, take this diamond ring.
You at least have it.
If you survive, you'll have it.
He didn't want to take it.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But then I begged him, because I knew I'm going to be gassed.
I didn't believe in what he's saying to me.
I said, you have it.
So he took it.
He said, I give it to you back.
You will see.
So I gave him the ring.
We were waiting there for hours.
And we begged them-- we ask the Germans, what-- what's happening?
They told us, it won't be too long.
Don't worry.
But we found out-- I don't know, look, if it's true or not, but it's what we were told-- that two days before, 2,000 Romanian girls came, and they were all gassed.
And that's why we are waiting, because they have to dispose the bodies so we can go in.
So we definitely knew that we're going to be finished.
While we were waiting people were still dropping dead, so they had less then.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Then in middle of the night, an order came that we're all going in to have a shower.
We didn't believe them, because we knew this is what they say usually when people are going to be gassed.
We didn't believe them that we're going to have a shower.
So we went to have a shower.
They asked us to be undressed, and I went into the shower.
The only thing you can leave-- if you have a belt with you, you can hold your belt on you.
We-- we didn't know what the belt is for or anything.
What do you need a belt for?
You haven't got any clothes.
What do you need a belt for?
So we went in to have the shower.
We looked if there is water going to come or there's going gas to come.
But we were very happy.
We found out there is water.
So we hoped maybe something is going to happen.
Maybe they-- they're going to look after us.
After we had the shower, if someone didn't have a belt, they gave him a belt.
And we couldn't understand it.
So-- and then naked we were walking, and we're supposed to go into a Krankenlager.
A Krankenlager is a hospital.
That was in the camp.
As we walked into the camp-- and they give me a number that-- to remember, and my number was 111.
That was my number.
And this is now-- I call whatever my luck is 111.
This is what I call-- because I don't believe in anything else.
But in 111, I do believe.
So we were walking to this one.
We-- we opened the door to this Krakenlager.
There were bodies lying everywhere.
Everywhere there were bodies.
You could even see that some pieces were missing from the bodies, because people were cutting and eating it.
So we knew where we were.
And we were told straightaway, there are the beds.
Your belt-- you see the hook there?
There was three people, one bed, a single bed like this for six people.
Three this way, three this way.
The belt was to put your head on it, because there wasn't enough room in the bed for the head.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
Toilet facilities-- not allowed to go a whole week to a toilet, because they knew you won't survive longer than a week.
You're not allowed to go anywhere.
Everything is done in bed.
You will have a shower after a week-- once a week, because they were sure no one is going to survive more than a week.
That was the first day there.
The second day, I was-- I just couldn't look at myself.
I wasn't-- I wasn't a human being.
I-- I was an animal.
I had shit all over me.
I wasn't human at all.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was thinking, what can I do?
How can I finish myself off?
What-- what can I do with myself?
I was thinking of my diamond.
I said to myself, maybe-- because I could see there were some people in those barracks that they had uniforms, not naked.
They were cleaning the place, and they were allowed to have a uniform.
And they were passing on your bread or whatever for the people.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I said, why, maybe I should go to see this fellow who's-- looks after this barrack.
He's the main boss.
He-- he was a German who didn't-- he was sent to the concentration camp because he didn't believe in Hitler as well.
And maybe he could help me.
Maybe he will go for the diamond, because it was a really huge diamond.
When I picked it up, it was just beautiful.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what I decided to do, because I had no alternative.
Though, maybe he will kill me for it.
Many people ?] do.
I didn't know what he's going to do, but I had nothing to lose anymore.
I-- I could see I'm finished.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
So at night, I was naked.
And he had-- near the gate, he had a room for himself.
I went into him, and I started speaking to him in German.
I was crying.
And I explain to him, I said, look, [INAUDIBLE] I don't know, but I just can't live like this.
I've got something which is absolutely beautiful.
If you could help me some way, in any way you can help me, I give you this thing.
He said, what is it?
I said, a diamond, and it's the most beautiful thing you ever seen.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said, where have you got it?
Show me.
I said, I haven't got it here.
I gave it to a friend who recognized me in the camp.
He used to work once at the crematory.
He says, don't worry.
Tomorrow morning, I'll let you go and have a shower, and I bring you clothes, and we go there together.
Well, I didn't know to believe him or not to believe him.
I had to go back to my bed, because you were not allowed to stand anywhere, just to be in bed.
I went back to my bed.
And I could see in the morning, he's coming with the clothes and everything.
And he said, go out, and you can have a shower.
So I went and washed myself.
I just couldn't believe it that, really, it's-- it's true.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He gave me the-- the uniform, and I was only hoping that I find this boy that he can give me back the diamond.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we went there.
And-- and as we came closer, and I saw the boy.
And he says, I know him.
We were working together at the crematorium.
This German, this bloke, I-- [? they ?] were once working with him together in the crematorium.
And when they saw each other, they kissed each other.
So they were very close together.
And he said, don't worry.
I will look after Maury.
Don't worry now, before even anything.
He didn't tell him that he's going-- I am going to give him the diamond or anything.
But I then said to the boy-- I explained to him everything, and he gave me the diamond.
He gave me the diamond.
I put it in my pocket, and then I-- when we came there, I give him the diamond.
What he did-- you see, those people who were cleaning the barracks had some-- something to do.
They were allowed to be in clothes, and they-- they had a separate bed to sleep for themselves.
And this is what happened to me.
He gave me a bed next to his room, not far.
And he-- what he used to do at night, he used to, when no one saw, he used to come and put things into my bed to eat, and I ate.
So I got a bit of strength then, because he was doing it maybe every night.
Then suddenly, he disappears.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And anybody I knew that came with me was nearly dead.
They were-- they were all dying because it was impossible to survive there.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And I couldn't understand.
I said to myself, who knows now what will happen to me?
Because I not getting any food.
But I had a better chance than the people did, because I was already in clothes So no one could take this away from me.
But I was afraid if it takes too long, and if he's not there, you know, I don't know where I'm going to finish up.
Then we could-- once they took us, and they-- they had a big gathering with everyone.
And we could see that the war is coming to an end.
We could hear it.
We smelled in the air that it was nearly finish.
And I was-- you know, the way he fed me, I-- I wasn't like the others.
I could walk.
I could, you know, I was-- I was already better, much better.
So we found out that this man went into hiding.

Because the Germans-- all those people who were working at crematoriums, they killed them all, every one of them, because-- so that they could no reference at all how many people were gassed, how many people died.
So they killed them.
As many as they found, they killed.
And he went to hiding.
But my friend who was working in crematorium, he tried to save my life.
[CRYING] And they got him, and they killed him.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So I survived, and-- and-- and he was shot.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
And then two days after, the Americans arrive.
Well, I can't tell you.
I-- I really can't explain.
But what the Germans did-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] those Germans who were guarding us were Gestapo at first, but they change the guard.
They put the Wehrmacht-- they put-- which they were just ordinary soldiers, they put them into guard.
And the Germans, I don't know what they did there.
I don't know why or whatever they did.
That was the 4th of May.
And the 5th of May, the-- the tank came in and a couple of jeeps, I-- if I can remember, to the camp.
And those who could walk or think ran to the gate, the main gate.
The Germans put their hands up, and they surrendered straightaway.
And the Americans came in.
When they came into this barrack, they just couldn't believe it.
People were clapping, bravo, and they put their heads down, they died.
There were bodies everywhere like.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And this is how I survive the war.
Yep.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then the Americans told us that the war is not finish yet-- that was 5th of May, 1945-- and that the war is still going on.
And those people who can guard, who are healthy enough to guard and do things to look after the camp, they have to go still back.
But everything around here will be guarded, not to worry.
Food was coming to the camp.
And they didn't realize that they're not doing any good with it.
Because people were starting to eat, and they were dying by the hundreds straightaway, you can even say thousands, because they couldn't digest the food.
So they realize-- after a few days they realize what's happening.
So they opened a Red Cross in the camp, and they were trying to start feeding like a child, like a baby you-- you feed.
And this is how some of them survived.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And this was my time in concentration camp.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]
After the war, I had a very good friend.
I met a very good friend-- [NON-ENGLISH] Silverberg, who is in Israel now, a very good friend of mine.
And he was one like I was.
He had a bed.
That's how he survive.
Otherwise, he wouldn't survive.
He was making the hats in Mauthausen, and that was his job.
Hats.
And that was his job.
He was younger than me.
He was one and  a half year younger than me.
And this is how we both-- we become very friendly.
And we didn't want to stay in the camp any longer.
So we tried to go outside the camp, and we found ourself a room with German people, Austrian people.
And then we heard that everyone should go back into the camp to get registered.
So I went to the camp with [NON-ENGLISH], and we registered ourself.
So I knock at the door, and a fellow comes in which I knew from the camp.
He was the kapo, but he was a very, very nice man.
His name was Katz after the war.
I didn't know his name before the-- during the time in-- in the camp.
No, he was a Jew.
But I-- he wasn't in the camp as a Jew.
He was as a German or as a Pole.
I don't know.
He was the nicest person that you could met.
Because usually, those kapos, there were some of them worse than the Germans.
And he was very nice, not only to the Jews, because with us there were non-Jews as well, and he was nice to everybody.
He tried to help you, and no one knew why or what.
He was just a nice man to us.
And when I knocked at the door, he came out.
I went to the Judenrate just to register myself.
And then when he came out, I said to him, look, I'm sorry, but it's not you that I want.
I'm looking for the Jewish Committee.
And he says, I am the one from the Jewish Committee.
And I am Jewish, and my name is Katz.
I just couldn't believe it.
Because if anyone would suspect something-- as you know, every Jew is circumcised.
And when we were in Mauthausen, I think when we had the shower we would had known.
But no one even dreamed that he's a Jew.
He was such a nice person.
And this is how I found out.
And I went in, and I got registered there.

But unfortunately, from what I heard-- look, I can't put 100% on it, but I just heard after the war, straightaway, two weeks after the war, that he was in Italy racing-- running on a motorbike, and he got killed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So--
Yeah.
It?
I hope it's not true.
I hope it only what I heard.
But this is what I heard from a few people.
From Mauthausen-- we were in Mauthausen for a little while.
And the Jewish Brigade came afterwards, and they took the list of all the young Jewish boys.
And they-- they really didn't ask us a lot, because, honestly, I must say, afterwards in Austria, well, all we knew was years in the camps.
We didn't know anything else.
But we had a terrific time.
The Austrians were giving us food.
They were giving us everything.
You could take even if you wanted to because the first month you had a free hand.
You could do whatever you wanted.
No one-- not that we did anything wrong.
But you could do-- they didn't say anything.
Only after a months, the big signs were that you got to be careful.
You're not to-- to take any revenges or anything.
But we had really a beautiful time in Austria, so we didn't mind staying.
But when they came and they told us we're going to Italy, oh, well, I wanted to go to little Poland.
But what we were told that in Poland-- if I come back to Poland that the Russians-- anybody who was in a camp, they can take you back to Siberia.
They heard rumors that were going in in Austria.
So I said, I went through hell and now to go back to Poland.
So I went to Italy.
And I didn't hear anything about my brother and sister.
So there was no mail going.
I couldn't find anything out.
So I went to Italy.
We-- we came to Italy to a place called Santa Maria.
And a matter of fact, we came in first to another place, which-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't remember the name of it.
But here is someone in Sydney who was working there in this camp.
That's Mr.
Moses.
And he was the one who picked me up there.
Now, he's a [? bit ?] related to me--
--here through my brother-in-law, late brother-in-law.
So-- and from there, there, when-- while I was there, I found they had a list of [?
Hayalim ?] who were in the English Army.
And all the list, I could--
[INAUDIBLE]?
Hayalim? ?] Soldiers.
They are Jewish soldiers who were in the brigade, in the Jewish Brigade, but in England.
And there was a list of names, and there I could see someone from my city is there.
So I went to the board, and I asked them if they can get in touch with-- if this man can get in touch with me.
It didn't take long, maybe an hour, and he was there with parcels and everything, with clothes for me.
And I had a uniform, a English uniform, and-- I think, and-- and-- whatever I wanted to eat and money and everything.
And he really-- and he was the one who managed to throw in near the border in Poland a Polish letter.
And this is the letter my sister received, and she knew that I'm alive.
But I didn't know that she knew this, because I left.
We were in-- from there, we went to Mauthausen to-- sorry, to-- to Santa Maria in Italy.
And in Santa Maria, we had a beautiful time.
We-- we lived in the villas what Mussolini used to have.
So we lived there for a few months, and we had a terrific time there.
The [?
Hayalim, ?] the soldiers, were looking after us.
They were feeding us and giving us the most beautiful time, singing and dancing, and-- and the best time we could ever have as kids.
Whatever I missed out--
Well, there I was already 18.
But we had a terrific time-- boys and girls together.
We had a really lovely time.
Which one?
The
Yes, in-- in Buchenwald, he got killed, yes.
They were running afterwards south from Buchenwald  or something, and he was killed.
Yes.
So I-- we had a beautiful time.
But then the English people, they've got, I think, 1,000 visas for children to go to Israel.
Because you couldn't go then legally to Israel.
Mostly they used to go illegal in those days.
And I was the one which, with them, that we went-- 1,000 kids or something-- went on the ship.
And I didn't know what to do.
So but-- I didn't have anyone.
I didn't know about my sister or brother anything.
So I-- I just went to Israel.
I wasn't so happy, really, because I didn't know if I made the right decision, because I wanted still to see.
I thought from Italy I might be able to find out quicker than from Israel.
So I didn't know what to do.
But I already went, and I had to make the best of it.
The friends were singing and dancing on the ship, and we arrived to Israel.
Though, ?] really, it was a beautiful thing the way they welcomed us.
People were waiting, and the orchestra was playing, and they were singing to kids.
And they really gave us a beautiful time-- a really beautiful time.
And then they took us to a school.
The school was called Magdiel.
It was not far from Tel Aviv.
And we used to teach Hebrew there for half a day, a few hours.
And-- and then half a day we used to work there.
And I was staying there with my friend.
I was staying there for a while.
And then after a few months, we left, and we went into Tel Aviv.
And I lived with a family in Tel Aviv.
And then I and my friend, we lived in one room.
We rented a room, and we lived together till I left Tel Aviv.
But while I was in Tel Aviv, I found out about my sister.
And--
--she was so happy.
And I was in touch whole time.
And they sent me then money.
And they wanted me to come as soon as possible, which I was very happy to do it.
So I left my friend, and I went back to Poland.
In Poland-- when I arrived to Czechoslovakia, I just couldn't believe that it's happening to me.
I just-- you know, I-- I couldn't believe it's possible.
When I arrived in Prague, I rang up my sister, and we-- we were so excited we-- we hardly could speak to each other.
And my brother-in-law then [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was waiting at the station for me.
I didn't know him, because they got married in Krakow after the war.
I didn't know him at all.
But I just happened-- as soon as the the train stopped, I looked and next to me was someone standing.
And I said, are you so and so?
And he says, yes.
So he hugged me and kissed me.
I never knew this man.
And-- and this is way I arrived to my sister.
My sister had a-- a child.
We were both very excited and very happy.
Happy.
My sister made a most beautiful dinner, and she had some visitors.
And I couldn't touch anything.
I just couldn't swallow anything.
I was so excited.
I just couldn't touch anything.
And I been in-- in Poland for about a year.
And I had a good time in Poland, because I wanted to make the best of it.
I survived the war, and in Poland my sister was well off.
So I had money, I had-- and I went skiing.
And-- because my sister-- I was due-- when I was in Poland, I was due to the army.
When you're 21, you have to join the army, but they call you earlier.
As soon as I arrived at Poland, I had a Polish passport, so they called me in.
And then I went in, and I told them that I'm here only on a short time-- for a short time, and I'm going back.
And they told me if you are not going back for such and such time, I-- you have to join in the army.
So I knew I'm-- I'm not going anywhere.
So the passport, in the meantime--
--expired.
I didn't know what to do with myself.
So I went-- I went skiing.
When I went skiing, I could see I was near the border, near the Czech border.
And I can see that it's not so hard to go through the border.
So I came back to my sister, and I told her that I think that I'm going to go to Austria.
